title: Wuthering Heights
author: Emily Bronte
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Wuthering Heights" book by Emily Bronte?
Options:
A. I'll read it, for I keep it yet. Any relic of the dead is precious if they were valued living. DEAR ELLEN, it begins, I came last night to Wuther- ing Heights, and heard for the first time that Catherine has been, and is yet, very ill. I must not write to her, I suppose, and my brother is either too angry or too dis- tressed to answer what I sent him. Still, I must write to somebody, and the only choice left me is you. Inform Edgar that I'd give the world to see his face again---that my heart returned to Thrushcross Grange in twenty-four hours after I left it, and is there at this moment, full of warm feelings for him and Catherine.
B. This letter is precious to me because it is a relic of one who has passed, if they were valued in life. Ellen, my dear, it begins, last night I came to Wuthering Heights and for the first time learned that Catherine has been, and still is, very ill. I suppose I should not write to her, and my brother is too distressed or angry to answer what I sent to him. Still, I must write to someone, and you are the only choice I have left. Let Edgar know that I would give the world to see his face again - that my heart returned to Thrushcross Grange 24 hours after leaving it, and is there now, full of affection for both him and Catherine.
C. I have kept this letter, for any memento of the departed is precious if they were cherished in life. My dear Ellen, it starts, last night I arrived at Wuthering Heights and first heard that Catherine has been, and still is, very sick. I assume I must not write to her, and my brother is either too upset or too angry to respond to what I sent him. However, I must write to someone, and you are the only option left for me. Tell Edgar that I would give anything to see his face again - that my heart went back to Thrushcross Grange 24 hours after leaving it, and is there now, full of warm feelings for both him and Catherine.
D. I've kept this letter, since any memento of someone now dead is precious if they were cherished alive. My dear Ellen, it starts, last night I arrived at Wuthering Heights and first heard that Catherine has been, and continues to be, very ill. I assume I cannot write to her, and my brother is too upset or angry to reply to what I sent him. However, I must write to someone, and you're the only option I have left. Inform Edgar that I would give anything to see his face again - that my heart went back to Thrushcross Grange 24 hours after leaving it, and is there now, filled with warm feelings for both him and Catherine.
Answer:
